edges.description: Excess glycolytic flux raises lactate.
Lactate accumulation produces lactic acidosis.
Increased de novo lipogenesis raises serum triglycerides.
Elevated serum triglycerides are the clinical biochemical phenotype.
Increased lipid synthesis raises serum cholesterol.
Elevated triglycerides and cholesterol manifest as hyperlipidemia.
Severe lipid elevation can manifest as cutaneous xanthomas.
Diarrhea can occur in GSD I, although the cached clinical summary does not resolve the intermediate mechanism.
Severe hypertriglyceridemia is a known intermediate for pancreatitis in untreated disease.
Purine degradation and lactate-impaired renal urate clearance raise serum uric acid.
Elevated serum uric acid clinically manifests as hyperuricemia.
Chronic hyperuricemia predisposes to gout.
Poor metabolic control is associated with liver adenoma risk.
Poor metabolic control is associated with low bone density and osteoporosis.
Poor metabolic control is associated with kidney disease that can present with albuminuria/proteinuria.
Chronic kidney involvement can progress to renal insufficiency.
Long-term follow-up associated anemia and other complications with suboptimal metabolic control.
Chronic metabolic disturbance is associated with impaired growth.
Untreated chronic metabolic disease is associated with delayed puberty.
Hypertension is a long-term complication of untreated GSD I, with intermediate mechanisms unresolved in the cached evidence.
Pulmonary hypertension is a long-term complication of untreated GSD I, with intermediate mechanisms unresolved in the cached evidence.
Neutrophil apoptosis and impaired homeostasis produce chronic neutropenia in GSD Ib.

nodes.description: The SGLT2 inhibitor empagliflozin has emerged as a mechanism-based treatment for symptoms caused by neutropenia and neutrophil dysfunction in GSD Ib, including mucosal lesions and inflammatory bowel disease. International consensus recommends a starting dose of 0.3-0.4 mg/kg/day.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment recommendations for glycogen storage disease type IB- associated neutropenia and neutrophil dysfunction with empagliflozin: Consensus from an international workshop.
evidence.snippet: Since 2019, the SGLT2-inhibitor empagliflozin has provided a mechanism-based treatment option for the symptoms caused by neutropenia/neutrophil dysfunction (e.g. mucosal lesions, inflammatory bowel disease).

evidence.reference_title: Treatment recommendations for glycogen storage disease type IB- associated neutropenia and neutrophil dysfunction with empagliflozin: Consensus from an international workshop.
evidence.snippet: We recommend to start empagliflozin in all GSD Ib individuals with clinical or laboratory signs related to neutropenia/neutrophil dysfunction with a dose of 0.3-0.4 mg/kg/d given as a single dose in the morning.
Impaired platelet function and development of reduced or dysfunctional von Willebrand factor can lead to a bleeding tendency with frequent epistaxis and menorrhagia in females.

Severely affected infants present in the neonatal period with severe hypoglycemia due to fasting intolerance. More commonly, untreated infants present at age three to four months with hepatomegaly, severe hypoglycemia with or without seizures, lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, and hypertriglyceridemia.
The diagnosis of GSD I is established in a proband by identification of biallelic pathogenic variants in either G6PC1 (GSD Ia) or SLC37A4 (GSD Ib).
G6PC1 | HGNC:4056 | glycogen storage disease I | MONDO:0002413 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Adeno-associated virus (AAV)-based gene therapy is under investigation for both GSD Ia and GSD Ib. Preclinical studies in mouse models demonstrate that liver-directed gene transfer corrects metabolic abnormalities and prevents hepatocellular adenoma development.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular biology and gene therapy for glycogen storage disease type Ib.
evidence.snippet: The results demonstrate that the liver-directed gene transfer and expression safely corrects metabolic abnormalities and prevents hepatocellular adenoma (HCA) development.

Glycogen storage disease type I (GSD I) is characterized by accumulation of glycogen and fat in the liver and kidneys resulting in hepatomegaly and nephromegaly.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type I: pathophysiology of liver adenomas.
evidence.snippet: Hepatic adenomas occur in 22%-75% of affected adults, according to the population studied, and from those reported in the literature are thought to have an approximately 10% risk of undergoing malignant transformation.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of continuous glucose therapy with uncooked cornstarch on the long-term clinical course of type 1a glycogen storage disease.
evidence.snippet: Of all subjects, 50% had at least one focal hepatic lesion consistent with an adenoma.

nodes.description: Hepatocellular adenomas are a major long-term complication of GSD I, occurring in 22-75% of affected adults. The adenomas develop typically in post-pubertal patients and carry an approximately 10% risk of malignant transformation to hepatocellular carcinoma. The pathogenesis is multifactorial and may involve chronic metabolic stress, altered hepatocyte growth signaling, and hormonal influences. Better dietary management and metabolic control appear to reduce adenoma incidence.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type I: pathophysiology of liver adenomas.
evidence.snippet: Hepatic adenomas occur in 22%-75% of affected adults, according to the population studied, and from those reported in the literature are thought to have an approximately 10% risk of undergoing malignant transformation.

evidence.snippet: the incidence of liver tumours in younger adults seems less than in older ones, suggesting that better dietary treatment, and thus improved metabolic control, may be protective.

nodes.description: Deficiency of the glucose-6-phosphatase system blocks the final step of both glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis, preventing the liver and kidneys from releasing free glucose into the bloodstream during fasting. Glucose-6-phosphate accumulates in the cytoplasm of hepatocytes and renal tubular cells, driving excessive glycogen and fat synthesis. The inability to maintain blood glucose between meals causes severe fasting hypoglycemia, which is the hallmark metabolic derangement of GSD I.

evidence.reference_title: Neutropenia in type Ib glycogen storage disease.
evidence.snippet: Glycogen storage disease type Ib, characterized by disturbed glucose homeostasis, neutropenia, and neutrophil dysfunction, is caused by a deficiency in a ubiquitously expressed glucose-6-phosphate transporter (G6PT).

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type Ib: role of glucose-6-phosphate transporter in cell metabolism and function.
evidence.snippet: Glycogen storage disease type Ib (GSD-Ib) is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by hypoglycemia, excessive glycogen accumulation in the liver and kidney, neutropenia, neutrophil dysfunction, and inflammatory bowel disease.

nodes.description: In GSD Ib, deficiency of the ubiquitously expressed glucose-6-phosphate transporter (G6PT) impairs neutrophil energy homeostasis. Neutrophils express the G6PT/G6Pase-beta complex, which produces endogenous glucose for neutrophil function. Loss of G6PT activity leads to enhanced endoplasmic reticulum stress, oxidative stress, and apoptosis, underlying the neutropenia and neutrophil dysfunction characteristic of GSD Ib. This manifests as recurrent bacterial infections, oral and intestinal ulcers, and inflammatory bowel disease.
nodes.id: Neutropenia and neutrophil dysfunction in GSD Ib

evidence.reference_title: Neutropenia in type Ib glycogen storage disease.
evidence.snippet: Loss of G6PT activity leads to enhanced endoplasmic reticulum stress, oxidative stress, and apoptosis that underlie neutropenia and neutrophil dysfunction in glycogen storage disease type Ib.

evidence.reference_title: Glycogen storage disease type Ib: role of glucose-6-phosphate transporter in cell metabolism and function.
evidence.snippet: Glycogen storage disease type Ib (GSD-Ib) is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by hypoglycemia, excessive glycogen accumulation in the liver and kidney, neutropenia, neutrophil dysfunction, and inflammatory bowel disease. GSD-Ib is caused by a deficiency of glucose-6-phosphate transporter (G6PT).

Individuals with untreated GSD Ib are more likely to develop impaired neutrophil and monocyte function as well as chronic neutropenia resulting in recurrent bacterial infections, gingivitis, periodontitis, and genital and intestinal ulcers.
Long-term complications of untreated GSD I include short stature, osteoporosis, delayed puberty, renal disease

Long-term complications of untreated GSD I include short stature, osteoporosis, delayed puberty, renal disease (including proximal and distal renal tubular acidosis, renal stones, and kidney failure), gout, systemic hypertension, pulmonary hypertension, hepatic adenomas with potential for malignancy, pancreatitis

evidence.reference_title: Molecular biology and gene therapy for glycogen storage disease type Ib.
evidence.snippet: Glycogen storage disease type Ib (GSD-Ib) is caused by a deficiency in the ubiquitously expressed glucose-6-phosphate (G6P) transporter (G6PT or SLC37A4).

nodes.description: The block in glucose-6-phosphate hydrolysis leads to accumulation of metabolic intermediates, resulting in several secondary biochemical abnormalities. Excess glucose-6-phosphate is shunted into glycolysis, producing excess pyruvate and lactate (lactic acidosis). Increased acetyl-CoA flux drives de novo lipogenesis, causing hypertriglyceridemia and hypercholesterolemia. Accelerated purine degradation from excess glucose-6-phosphate metabolism, combined with decreased renal urate clearance due to competitive inhibition by lactate, leads to hyperuricemia.

evidence.reference_title: Hepatic glycogen storage disorders: what have we learned in recent years?
evidence.snippet: Evidence accumulates that poor metabolic control is a risk factor for the development of long-term complications, such as liver adenomas, low bone density/osteoporosis, and kidney disease in GSD I.

evidence.snippet: All of the complications were associated with evidence of suboptimal metabolic control, whereas subjects with no evidence of any long-term complications had near normal blood lactate and total CO(2) concentrations.

Long-term complications of untreated GSD I include short stature, osteoporosis, delayed puberty, renal disease (including proximal and distal renal tubular acidosis, renal stones, and kidney failure), gout, systemic hypertension, pulmonary hypertension

nodes.description: Dietary management with frequent meals of uncooked cornstarch (a slow-release glucose polymer) is the cornerstone of GSD I treatment. Cornstarch provides a sustained source of glucose to prevent fasting hypoglycemia. Patients require multiple doses per day including overnight. Combined with avoidance of fructose, galactose, and sucrose. Optimal metabolic control reduces long-term complications.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of continuous glucose therapy with uncooked cornstarch on the long-term clinical course of type 1a glycogen storage disease.
evidence.snippet: The achievement of optimal biochemical control of glycogen storage disease type 1a continues to be a challenge, but is attainable by meticulous adherence to an individualized dietary regimen based on the results of periodic metabolic evaluation and home blood glucose monitoring.
Xanthoma and diarrhea may be present.
